title: Add MCP tools for creating and updating bullets directly
description:
Currently there is no MCP tool for manually creating bullets — only forge_derive_bullets (AI-driven) and forge_update_bullet (edit existing). Manual bullet creation requires direct SQL INSERT into bullets + bullet_sources + bullet_skills tables.

Needed tools:
1. **forge_create_bullet** — Create a bullet with content, source_id, status (default in_review), optional domain/notes/metrics. Should auto-create bullet_sources link.
2. **forge_add_bullet_skill** — Add a skill link to a bullet (bullet_skills junction)
3. **forge_remove_bullet_skill** — Remove a skill link from a bullet

Context: During the 2026-04-07 data quality audit, 12 new bullets were created for Cisco (8) and Raytheon Cloud Forensics Engineer (4) roles. All required raw SQL because no MCP tool exists for manual bullet creation. This is a common workflow when interviewing users about their experience and creating bullets collaboratively.

Note: forge_remove_resume_skill and forge_remove_resume_entry already have a void-return serialization bug (TypeError on undefined). Ensure new tools return meaningful responses.
close_reason: Already implemented in tier3-bullets.ts — forge_create_bullet, forge_add_bullet_skill, forge_remove_bullet_skill all registered and wired through SDK + routes

title: Skills taxonomy audit — distribution, categorization, and gaps
description:
Review all Skills at /data/skills for:
1. **Category distribution**: Are skills evenly distributed or are some categories bloated (e.g. 'other') while others are thin?
2. **Multi-category skills**: Identify skills that could belong in multiple categories (e.g. 'Terraform' is in 'language' but could also be 'infrastructure'; 'MLFlow' is in 'infrastructure' but could be 'ai_ml').
3. **Category fitness**: For each category, review every member. Flag misplacements (e.g. 'RAG Pipelines' was in infrastructure, 'Linkerd' vs 'Service Mesh').
4. **Missing categories**: Are there natural groupings not represented? (e.g. 'cloud_platforms' vs lumping AWS/Azure/GCP into 'platform' with AppDynamics/Datadog).
5. **Missing skills**: Based on resume bullets and JD requirements, are there skills that should exist but don't?
Uses the new skill_categories table from migration 044.
close_reason: Reclassified all 14 'other' skills to proper categories (0 remaining in other). Key findings documented: 112 skills with no bullet links, 7 resume skills without backing, Cloud/AWS naming tangle — all deferred to dr4.

title: Clean up perspectives linked to rejected bullets
description:
After the bullet dedup (47 rejections), some rejected bullets still have approved or pending perspectives attached. For each rejected bullet:
1. Check if it has perspectives (approved, pending, or draft).
2. If the perspective content is unique and valuable, migrate it to the surviving bullet in the dedup cluster (the one we kept).
3. If the perspective is a duplicate of one already on the surviving bullet, reject it.
4. If the perspective has content not captured anywhere else, extract it as a note or new bullet before rejecting.
Goal: No approved perspectives should point to rejected bullets.
close_reason: Migrated all 43 orphaned perspectives to surviving bullets. 0 rejections needed — every perspective had a matching survivor. 16 resume entries across 4 resumes (Anthropic Agent Infra, Sandboxing, Public Sector, Snorkel AI) now point to valid bullets.

title: Domains and archetypes taxonomy audit
description:
Review all Domains at /data/domains and Archetypes for:
1. **Domain descriptions**: Current domains have brief descriptions. Expand with clear scope definitions and examples of what belongs in each.
2. **Domain completeness**: Are there missing domains? (e.g. 'mlops' appears as a bullet domain but not in the domains table; 'data-engineering' is used in bullets but may not be a formal domain).
3. **Archetype-domain mapping**: Review which domains each archetype expects. Are mappings complete? (e.g. 'ml-engineer' archetype doesn't exist — resume uses it but it's not in the archetypes table).
4. **Archetype completeness**: Are there missing archetypes for target roles? (e.g. 'ml-engineer', 'data-engineer', 'devops-engineer').
5. **Category fitness**: Same analysis as skills — do all domain members fit? Are there better groupings?
close_reason: Created ml-engineer/devops-engineer/research-engineer archetypes, dropped hft. Added descriptions to 5 domains. Remaining design questions (domain merges, empty-archetype resumes) deferred to dr4.

title: Audit roles for bullet overlap and coverage gaps
description:
Review all Roles at /experience/roles for:
1. **Cross-role overlap**: Compare bullets across roles at the same employer (especially Raytheon's 3 roles) and across employers. Identify where the same work is claimed under multiple roles and recommend which role should own it.
2. **Bullet coverage**: For each role, check if it has derived bullets. Flag roles with zero bullets or thin coverage (e.g. only 1-2 bullets for a multi-year role).
3. **Lineage integrity**: Verify role→bullet→perspective chains are intact. Flag orphaned bullets (no source) or roles whose bullets were rejected in dedup but not replaced.
Known issue: Raytheon Cloud Forensics Engineer and Principal Cloud Forensics Engineer share tool-evaluation and data-ingestion bullets.
close_reason: Audit complete. Cisco (3 bullets) and Raytheon Cloud Forensics Eng (5 bullets) flagged as thin — bullet derivation sessions planned. Principal near-dupes deferred. No zero-coverage roles found.

title: Audit projects for source data completeness and bullet coverage
description:
Review all Project sources at /experience/projects for:
1. **Source data completeness**: Many projects have thin descriptions (1-2 sentences). Expand descriptions with technical details, architecture decisions, and outcomes to support richer bullet derivation.
2. **Bullet coverage**: Check which projects have derived bullets vs which are still just a description. Flag projects with zero bullets.
3. **Cross-project overlap**: Some projects overlap with role work (e.g. MLOps pipeline exists as both a role bullet and a project source). Clarify ownership.
4. **Snorkel-relevant projects**: Sanskrit, Bespin, Forge, and LLM Agent Experiment Platform are on the current resume. Ensure their source data is rich enough to derive multiple perspective variants.
This will require significant investment in expanding project source descriptions.

title: Presentation bullet edits fail with 'Entry not found' — synthetic IDs not in DB
description: The resume compiler generates synthetic UUIDs (e.g. 752a6c59-752a-452a-a52a-752a6c590000) for presentation bullet sub-items. These IDs are returned in the compiled IR but don't exist in the resume_entries table. When the web UI tries to PATCH /api/resumes/:id/entries/:syntheticId, it gets 404 NOT_FOUND. The real entry is the parent presentation entry (e.g. c45a730f). Fix options: (A) Create real child entries for presentation bullets, (B) Route edits to the parent entry, (C) Make the UI aware of synthetic vs real IDs.

title: Extend perspectives to cover project sources
description: Currently project entries on resumes use direct content overrides on resume_entries, bypassing the bullet→perspective→resume_entry pipeline that experience entries use. Projects should support the same derivation flow: derive perspectives from project source descriptions, approve them, and add them to resumes via perspective_id. This would enable versioned, archetype-aware project descriptions and consistent provenance tracking.

title: Design: reusable error indicator + issue resolution modal

Two reusable design-system components deferred from the 2026-04-05 session:

1. Issue/Error Modal — surface page-level data problems (e.g. 'N unlinked role sources') with fast-path resolution actions. Reusable across sources, resumes, orgs pages.

2. Alert Indicator — inline warning bubble/badge on Entry components (padded, unpadded), inputs, selects, buttons. Reusable like a CSS class + Svelte component. Highlights when an entry has a problem (missing org, expired cert, etc).

Both should share visual language (severity levels, problem → resolution interaction pattern). Brainstorm together as a 'data quality indicator' design system spec.

- serde with custom deserializers for schema validation

Blocked until TS server is fully decommissioned (R1 complete).

title: Prompt template variablization — decouple prompts from binary releases
description:
Refactor forge-ai prompt templates from compiled-in constants to a pluggable template registry. Goal: prompt updates ship independently of application releases via a prompt distribution channel (prompt management registry, config service, or CDN).

Current state: templates are const strings in forge-ai source code, versioned with the binary. This means every prompt change requires a code change, rebuild, and deploy.

Target state: forge-ai loads templates from a registry interface (trait). Implementations:
- CompiledRegistry (default, current behavior — templates baked in as fallback)
- RemoteRegistry (fetches from prompt management service)
- FileRegistry (loads from disk, useful for local dev/testing)

Seams needed in initial implementation:
- Template rendering functions should take template content as input, not hardcode it
- Version constants should be metadata, not tied to the function
- The renderer is pure: (template + variables) → prompt string

This enables the Prompt Management initiative (forge-7dct) to own the template lifecycle without touching forge-ai code.

Depends on: forge-ai phase 1 landing first (establishes the template shapes).

title: Improve JD requirement parser — parser combinator rewrite
description: Replace the regex/heuristic JD requirement parser in forge-ai with a proper parser combinator implementation (nom or winnow). The initial port is a 1:1 translation of the TS regex-based parser for behavioral parity during strangler fig migration. Once the TS server is decommissioned, rewrite for: better accuracy on unstructured postings, confidence calibration, support for non-English JDs, structured error reporting on parse failures. Blocked until TS server is fully decommissioned (R1 complete).

title: Research — LLMOps platform evaluation (Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, etc.)
description:
Evaluate LLMOps platforms for prompt analysis and observability. Forge already uses MLflow for Claude Code telemetry (mitmproxy → JSONL → MLflow). Need to decide whether to extend MLflow or adopt a purpose-built LLMOps tool.

Candidates:
- Langfuse (OSS, self-hosted, OTEL-native, prompt management built-in, Rust SDK via REST)
- Arize Phoenix (OSS, self-hosted, trace visualization, evals framework)
- MLflow (already deployed, general-purpose, less LLM-specific)
- Helicone (managed, proxy-based, easy integration but vendor lock-in)
- Braintrust (managed, evals-focused, good prompt comparison)
- Promptfoo (OSS, CLI-first, eval/testing focused, no runtime tracing)
- LangSmith (managed, LangChain ecosystem, no self-hosted)
- Lilypad (OSS, decorators/annotations approach)
- Agenta (OSS, prompt playground + evaluation)

Evaluation criteria:
- Self-hosted option (required for dogfooding + data sovereignty)
- OTEL integration (traces should flow from existing OTEL pipeline)
- Rust SDK or REST API quality
- Prompt versioning built-in vs bolted-on
- Cost at small scale
- Eval/testing framework quality
- Production trace visualization

title: Prompt Management & LLMOps — versioning, promotion, attribution, and analysis
description:
Initiative for the prompt lifecycle and LLM observability layer. Sits between forge-ai (which renders prompt templates) and the AI Gateway (which routes API calls to providers).

Scope:

1. Prompt Versioning — templates have semantic versions, stored in a registry (DB or config). Each prompt template (source→bullet, bullet→perspective, JD skill extraction, future templates) is versioned independently. Changes are tracked with diffs.

2. Prompt Bundling — group related prompt versions into a coherent set (e.g., 'derivation bundle v2' = source→bullet-v2 + bullet→perspective-v3). Bundles are the unit of deployment.

3. Prompt Promotion — dev → staging → prod lifecycle for prompt changes. Integrated with feature flags so new prompt versions can be A/B tested or gradually rolled out per-tenant.

4. Prompt Attribution — every LLM output is tagged with the prompt template version, bundle version, and provider that produced it. Already partially implemented via prompt_logs table in TS codebase. Enables: debugging bad outputs, compliance auditing, quality regression detection.

5. Prompt Analysis & Observability — integration with an LLMOps platform for: cost/latency tracking per prompt version, quality scoring, drift detection, prompt comparison dashboards. Candidate platforms to evaluate: Langfuse (OSS, self-hosted), LangSmith, Lilypad, Agenta, PromptHub, Braintrust, PromptLayer, Vellum, Promptfoo, Arize Phoenix, MLflow (already used for Claude Code telemetry), Helicone.

Relationships:
- forge-ai renders prompts using templates from this registry
- AI Gateway routes the rendered prompt to an LLM provider
- This initiative tracks what was sent, what came back, and how well it worked
- OTEL integration (forge-mar5) provides the transport layer for traces/metrics
- Feature flags (forge-mgr2) gate prompt version rollouts

Non-goals for v1: automated prompt optimization, prompt fine-tuning, multi-modal prompt support.
notes: Children: forge-dkuz (LLMOps platform eval). Related: forge-9s98 (AI Gateway), forge-mar5 (OTEL), forge-mgr2 (feature flags). forge-ai renders templates from this registry.

title: AI Gateway — multi-provider LLM routing and edge MCP clients
description:
Initiative for the direct LLM API client layer. Forge's split-handshake model (server prepares prompts, client invokes LLM, client commits responses) works for MCP-connected users. For SaaS, the server needs to call LLMs directly.

Scope:
- Edge MCP clients via Cloudflare Workers (server-side prompt execution)
- Multi-provider routing: Anthropic, OpenAI, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI
- OSS/self-hosted support: Ollama, vLLM, local models via OpenAI-compatible API
- Provider selection logic (cost, latency, capability, user preference)
- API key management per-tenant (BYOK + platform keys)
- Rate limiting and cost tracking per-tenant per-provider
- Retry/fallback across providers
- Streaming response support

This is deliberately separated from forge-ai (which handles prompt templates, validators, embeddings, and JD parsing). forge-ai prepares what to send; the AI Gateway decides where to send it.

Non-goals for v1: fine-tuned model hosting, model training, prompt optimization/caching beyond provider-native.
notes: Related: forge-7dct (Prompt Management — what to send), forge-9s98 is where to send it. forge-ai sits between them (renders templates).

title: Decision — API rate limiting strategy
description:
Select a rate limiting approach for Forge SaaS API. Currently no rate limiting (single-user local app).

Scope:
- Per-tenant request rate limits (requests/min, requests/day)
- Per-endpoint tiering (read-heavy vs write vs AI-calling endpoints)
- AI endpoint special handling (derivations, alignment — expensive, should have lower limits)
- Rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After)

Options to evaluate:
(1) tower-governor — tower middleware, in-memory, uses governor crate (token bucket). Simple, no external deps.
(2) Redis-backed — distributed rate limiting via Redis (needed for multi-instance K8s). Crates: redis-rs + custom middleware.
(3) Hybrid — in-memory for single-instance, Redis for multi-instance (feature-flagged).

Evaluation criteria: single-instance vs distributed, Rust crate maturity, per-tenant keying, graceful degradation, integration with Axum middleware stack.

Depends on auth (need tenant identity to key rate limits).
notes: Logical dependency: depends on forge-j9by (auth epic — need tenant identity to key rate limits)

title: Rust Rewrite — incremental TS→RS migration (R0-R4)
description:
Incremental TS-to-Rust migration across R0-R4 plus R1.5 (forge-wasm). Strangler fig pattern — new Rust code runs alongside existing TS until parity.

## Revised Migration Timeline (browser-first)

R0 (DONE): forge-core types + forge-sdk repos/services — foundation Rust crates
R1 (in progress): Axum HTTP server — server-side Rust, strangler fig over Hono
R1.5 (NEW, critical path): forge-wasm — browser WASM data layer consuming R0 output. This is the crate that runs in browser, consumed by Svelte (now) and Dioxus (R4).
R2: Tauri desktop — Svelte frontend + Rust backend via Tauri, consuming forge-wasm
R3: MCP server port — server-side, independent of browser work
R4: Dioxus UI — replaces Svelte UI layer only. Data layer (forge-wasm) already built and tested. Scope is UI swap, not full rewrite.

## Key Architecture Decision (2026-04-27)

Browser-first with Rust WASM data layer, progressive UI migration:
- Svelte handles UI rendering (Phase 1-3, what its good at)
- Rust WASM handles compute (extraction, alignment, HNSW, graph traversal, wa-sqlite access)
- forge-wasm crate is built ONCE, consumed by Svelte (wasm-bindgen) then Dioxus (native)
- The expensive investment (data layer) survives every UI migration

See docs/src/architecture/models/deployment.md for full deployment architecture.

title: Phase 4: DuckPGQ Adapter
description:
Implement DuckPGQ adapter for read-only analytics over existing SQLite data. SQL/PGQ (ISO SQL:2023) property graph queries via DuckDB's ATTACH mechanism — zero migration for read path. Target: analytics dashboards, coverage scoring, alignment reports.

## Context
Phase 0-2 complete. The StorageAdapter interface supports read-only adapters (AnalyticsStore pattern). DuckPGQ uses CREATE PROPERTY GRAPH over ATTACH'd SQLite tables for graph pattern matching. Key unknown: does CREATE PROPERTY GRAPH work over ATTACH'd SQLite? Needs validation.
design: See spec: .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-08-storage-abstraction-spike-design.md (Phase 4 section)

- DuckPgqAdapter implements StorageAdapter (read-only subset)
- EntityStore is read-only via ATTACH SQLite + DuckDB queries
- GraphStore uses CREATE PROPERTY GRAPH + SQL/PGQ pattern matching
- VectorStore not implemented (or delegates to DuckDB vss extension)
- AnalyticsStore uses DuckDB columnar engine for aggregation/scoring
- Validates ATTACH'd SQLite interop (critical unknown)
- Property graph DDL covers Forge schema (vertices + edges)

title: Phase 3: GraphQLite Adapter
description:
Implement GraphQLite adapter for OSS/self-hosted Forge deployments. Cypher-based graph overlay on existing SQLite file — zero infra, single-file deployment. Pairs with sqlite-vec for vector search.

## Context
Phase 0 (adapter interface), Phase 1 (SQLite adapter), and Phase 2 (HelixDB adapter) are complete and merged. The StorageAdapter interface and EntityLifecycleManager are stable. This adapter delegates EntityStore to SQLite and adds GraphStore via Cypher queries through the GraphQLite SQLite extension.
design: See spec: .claude/plans/forge-resume-builder/refs/specs/2026-04-08-storage-abstraction-spike-design.md (Phase 3 section)
acceptance_criteria:
- GraphQLiteAdapter implements StorageAdapter interface
- EntityStore delegates to SQLite (relational tables are source of truth)
- GraphStore uses Cypher via SELECT cypher(...) over GraphQLite extension
- VectorStore delegates to sqlite-vec extension (if available) or brute-force fallback
- Extension loading works on macOS and Linux
- Graph sync mechanism mirrors FK/junction data as GraphQLite edges
- Integration tests pass against real GraphQLite extension
- Round-trip migration test: sqlite → graphqlite → sqlite

title: Resume compiler profile fetch refactor
description:
## Original Goal

The resume compiler at `packages/core/src/services/resume-compiler.ts` ~line 117 does 3 raw SQL queries to fetch the user profile, then manually JOINs address + URLs:

```ts
const profileRow = db.query('SELECT * FROM user_profile LIMIT 1').get()
const address = db.query('SELECT * FROM addresses WHERE id = ?').get(profileRow.address_id)
const urls = db.query('SELECT * FROM profile_urls WHERE profile_id = ? ORDER BY position').all(profileRow.id)
profile = { ...profileRow, address, urls }
```

This duplicates the exact logic in `ProfileService.fetchSingleton()`. Goal: eliminate the duplication.

## Current Context

**Compiler is sync, ProfileService is async.** `compileResumeIR(db, resumeId, elm?)` is a synchronous function. `ProfileService.getProfile()` returns `Promise<Result<UserProfile>>` (ELM is async). Can't call it without making the compiler async.

**Making the compiler async is high-churn.** ~70 test call sites + 5 production callers (ResumeService, ExportService) would need `await`. Callers are already async, but tests all call `compileResumeIR(db, resumeId)` synchronously.

**The compiler already has an optional `elm` param** but doesn't use it for profile fetching.

## Options Identified

**A) Full async conversion** — Make compiler async, use ProfileService.getProfile(). High churn (~70 test sites), but fully eliminates raw SQL for profile.

**B) Sync helper extraction** — Extract the 3 raw SQL queries into a `fetchProfileSync(db)` helper. Keeps compiler sync, reduces inline mess, but still duplicates ProfileService logic.

**C) Accept the duplication** — The raw SQL works, is tested, and is unlikely to change. The "refactor" may not be worth the churn. Revisit when the compiler gets a larger async rewrite (Phase 2 HelixDB work will force this anyway).

## Decision Needed

Have a conversation to decide which option (or a hybrid) is the right trade-off given the Phase 2 HelixDB work on the horizon.

title: Extension: in-memory session cache for org resolution + URL dedup
description:
Add lightweight in-memory caching in the background service worker to avoid redundant API calls during a session.

**What to cache:**
- Org search results: Map<companyName, orgId> — same company name → same org within a session
- Org creation results: add to org cache after create
- URL dedup positive hits: Set<canonicalUrl> — known captured URLs return 'Already captured' instantly

**Implementation:**
- Simple Map/Set in service worker memory (not chrome.storage.local)
- Cleared automatically on service worker restart (correct TTL = session)
- ~2KB for 50 entries — negligible memory impact
- Cross-browser safe (Map/Set work in all browsers)

**What NOT to cache:**
- Health check (must reflect real-time state)
- Negative lookupByUrl results (JD might be created via webui between checks)
- Full org list (large, changes frequently)
- JD creation results (write operation, no re-read needed)

**Performance impact:**
- Second click on same page → instant 'Already captured' (no HTTP)
- Multiple JDs from same company → org resolution from cache (skip GET /api/organizations)
- Reduces 2-4 HTTP round trips to 0-1 for repeat operations

title: Bullet-Skill Implicit Tagger Model
description:
Train a model that identifies latent/implicit skills in bullet text without explicit tagging. 'Managed production K8s clusters serving 10M req/day' should output [Kubernetes, Production Operations, High-Scale Systems, SRE] even without any being explicitly tagged.

## Why a Model

Current approach: skills are explicitly tagged on bullets by the user or extracted by LLM. Many skills are IMPLICIT in bullet text but never tagged. A dedicated model would:
- Read bullet text
- Output a ranked list of skills (from the skill graph) that the bullet demonstrates
- Confidence scores for each
- Works without LLM inference (small model, browser-compatible)

## Training Data

- Existing bullet → skill tag pairs in Forge (positive examples)
- LLM-generated labels on untagged bullets (silver labels, validated by human review)
- Skill graph provides the label space (output is skill graph node IDs)

## Architecture Options

- Multi-label classification on top of sentence embeddings
- Retrieval-based: embed bullet → HNSW match against skill embeddings → re-rank
- Hybrid: classification head for common skills + retrieval for rare skills

## Priority

P4 — after production. Current explicit tagging + LLM extraction is sufficient for MVP.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Model identifies 80%+ of human-tagged skills from bullet text alone
- [ ] Produces relevant implicit skills not explicitly tagged (validated by human review)
- [ ] Confidence scores correlate with human judgment
- [ ] Runs in browser via transformers.js
- [ ] Model weights < 30MB

title: Skill Embedding Model v2 (structured inputs)
description:
Train a custom skill embedding model that uses structured metadata (category, aliases, co-occurrence context, graph neighbors) as input features, not just skill name text. Produces higher-quality embeddings for the skill graph HNSW index.

## Why Custom Embeddings

Generic sentence embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) embed skill NAMES as text. But 'Python' the language and 'Python' the snake are identical as text. Structured inputs resolve this:
- Skill name + category + aliases + top co-occurring skills as input
- Model learns that 'Python [language, programming, data-science, machine-learning]' is different from 'Python [animal, reptile, snake]'

## Training Approach

- Contrastive learning on skill pairs from the curated skill graph
- Positive pairs: alias-of, child-of, related-to edges
- Hard negatives: skills with similar names but different categories
- Training data: curated skill graph (10k+ nodes with typed edges)

## Constraints

- Output must be compatible with HNSW index (fixed-dimension vectors)
- Must run in browser via transformers.js for inference
- Training is server-side (can use full GPU)
- Model weights < 30MB (smaller than generic MiniLM since domain-specific)

## Priority

P4 — after production. Generic MiniLM embeddings are sufficient for MVP. This improves quality at scale.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Custom model produces embeddings that cluster same-category skills more tightly than MiniLM
- [ ] Disambiguation: 'Python (language)' and 'Python (framework)' are separated in embedding space
- [ ] HNSW recall@10 improves over MiniLM baseline on skill matching benchmark
- [ ] Runs in browser via transformers.js
- [ ] Model weights < 30MB

title: JD Component Extraction Model (transformers.js)
description:
Train or fine-tune a small model for JD section identification that runs in-browser via transformers.js. Replaces current regex/heuristic section parsing with a learned model.

## What This Model Does

Given raw JD text, classify sections into: RoleResponsibilities, RoleReqsHard, RoleReqsSoft, RolePreferences, RoleDescriptionProse, RoleTeamDescription, RoleSalaryRange, RoleLearnMore, OrgAboutUs, OrgBenefits, OrgDisclaimers, etc.

## Why a Model (not just regex)

JD formats vary wildly across companies. Regex catches common patterns but fails on:
- Unlabeled sections (requirements listed without a 'Requirements' header)
- Non-standard headers ('What You Bring' vs 'Requirements')
- Mixed sections (benefits embedded in description)
- Bullet lists without section context

## Constraints

- Must run in browser via transformers.js (WASM/WebGPU)
- Must be small enough for reasonable browser download (< 50MB model weights)
- Must be fast enough for interactive use (< 1s per JD)
- Training data: existing JDs in Forge + public JD datasets

## Priority

P3 — after ALL production development is complete. Current heuristic parser (forge-3bp.21) is sufficient for MVP.

## Connects To

- forge-f3iq (JD Description automate section identification)
- forge-3bp.21 (Extension JD Structural Parser)
- forge-czgq (Extraction Pipeline — section-aware extraction strategy)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Model trained on 500+ labeled JD sections
- [ ] Accuracy > 85% on held-out test set
- [ ] Runs in browser via transformers.js in < 1s per JD
- [ ] Model weights < 50MB
- [ ] Integrated into extraction pipeline as section pre-processor

title: L3: Small model fallback for ambiguous sections (optional)
description:
For sections that L1+L2 classify with 'low' confidence, run through a small model for classification. Two options to evaluate:

Option A: Fine-tuned MiniLM/DistilBERT (~50MB ONNX) - fast, browser-compatible via ONNX Runtime Web (WASM). Needs training data (~500-1000 labeled JD sections).

Option B: Qwen2.5-0.5B via WebLLM (~300MB) - general purpose, no training data needed, but slower and larger. Uses WebGPU.

This is an enhancement layer - L1+L2 should handle 80-90% of JDs without it. Only build if the heuristic approach proves insufficient.

Decision gate: After L1+L2 are implemented, measure classification accuracy across 50+ real JDs. If accuracy is >90%, skip L3.
